one_line_takeaway: A compositional, interpretable multi-metric 'fingerprint' toolkit for diagnosing German LLM text-simplification behavior, validated via classifier-based meta-evaluation (up to F1 71.9 for the 1B-model label) rather than correlation with human judgments; this is an NLP evaluation-methodology paper with no security, safety, or adversarial content.
key_finding: The full Profiler feature set substantially outperforms both baselines across nearly all target labels (Table 2). Reported Accuracy/F1 (mean ± std) for the full feature set vs. Simple Baseline vs. Random: Plain 84.4±0.7 / 39.1±1.9 (Simple: 83.7±0.7/35.2±2.1; Random: 71.9±0.9/16.9±2.0); Target 86.6±0.4 / 48.2±1.7 (Simple: 83.2±0.0/0.0±0.0; Random: 71.8±0.2/16.4±1.4); Rules 78.9±0.5 / 64.6±1.1 (Simple: 78.0±0.8/62.4±1.6; Random: 55.6±0.5/33.6±0.5); Content 80.7±0.5 / 67.1±1.0 (Simple: 78.0±0.6/61.1±1.2; Random: 55.6±0.6/33.5±0.9); 1B 81.7±0.2 / 71.9±0.3 (Simple: 77.7±0.5/65.7±0.7; Random: 54.8±0.6/32.4±0.9); 4B 66.4±0.2 / 3.4±1.1 (Simple: 66.6±0.0/0.0±0.0; Random: 55.2±0.5/32.9±0.8); 12B 80.1±1.1 / 67.0±1.8 (Simple: 78.6±1.7/63.5±3.0; Random: 55.9±0.6/33.9±1.0). The abstract's claimed 'F1-scores up to 71.9%, improving upon baselines by over 48 percentage points' corresponds to the 1B-model F1 of 71.9 vs. the Random baseline F1 of 32.4 for that row (a delta of ~39.5pp on that specific row; the paper's largest F1 gain versus the Simple Baseline specifically is the Target label, 48.2 vs 0.0, i.e. the '48.3%' figure cited in the text). The 4B model classification was near-failure (F1 3.4), attributed by the authors to a 'middle-ground problem' for the linear classifier (4B sits between 1B and 12B), not to indistinct fingerprints — supported by pairwise classification (Table 3) showing strong separability: 1B vs 4B Acc 80.25/F1 79.77, 4B vs 12B Acc 68.96/F1 68.96, 1B vs 12B Acc 86.57/F1 86.04, all clearly above their respective Simple/Random baselines. Few-shot sensitivity (Table 4): Rules FS-vs-NoFS Acc 63.22±1.5/F1 61.77±1.5; Content Acc 77.30±1.6/F1 77.30±1.5, both above baselines, showing the fingerprint is sensitive to subtle prompt-engineering changes. Ablation (Table 5) removing Average Sentence Length (ASL) notably hurts Target (F1 48.3→24.9) and Rules (F1 64.6→52.1) detection, while leaving Content, 1B, 4B roughly unchanged, indicating ASL is a key but non-obvious discriminative feature (feature-importance heatmap in Fig. 2 shows FBR/COV are the key discriminators for Target prompts, LEN for the 1B model).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'Simplification Profiler', a diagnostic toolkit for German Automatic Text Simplification (ATS) that produces a multidimensional, interpretable 'fingerprint' of a simplification model's outputs. Instead of a single monolithic quality score (e.g. BLEU/SARI or learnable metrics like LENS/REFeREE), it decomposes evaluation into 9 core metrics (COR, COV, SIM, LNG, FBR, COH, LEN, ENT, ASL) plus additional sub-features (23 total features used in classification), spanning semantic fidelity (NLI-based content correctness and coverage), linguistic quality (rule-based simplicity/correctness via LanguageTool, a German readability index FBR, sentence-embedding coherence), and simple heuristics (text length, named-entity ratio, average sentence length). The toolkit is validated via a meta-evaluation: training a linear (LogisticRegression) classifier on the fingerprint features to predict which model/prompt configuration produced a given simplification, on the premise that if the fingerprint is descriptive, a linear classifier should be able to recover the generating condition. The authors position this as bypassing the need for large human-rated datasets, which are scarce for German ATS. Their stated 'main technical contribution' is the open-source implementation (GitHub: MSLars/German-Simplification-Profiling).
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a text-simplification evaluation/NLP-tooling paper with no adversary, attacker capability, or security threat model of any kind; it does not address model security, robustness to attack, privacy, or misuse.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) The Profiler is a differential/diagnostic tool for comparing System A vs. System B, not a substitute for holistic human quality judgment; correlating fingerprints with human preference for specific user groups is left as future work. (2) Compositional design means effectiveness is bounded by constituent components — COR/COV rely entirely on an NLI model whose biases/domain gaps propagate into scores; COH (cosine similarity of adjacent sentence embeddings) is only a heuristic for local coherence and may penalize valid restructuring; SIM/LNG are limited by LanguageTool's rule coverage. (3) Validation is limited to German Wikipedia (encyclopedic, fact-driven domain); porting to other languages/domains is non-trivial due to language-specific tools (FBR formula, German LanguageTool rules) and may require different evaluation dimensions. (4) The feature set is not exhaustive — tone/register appropriateness, user engagement, and trust are not captured. (5) The 4B model classification failure in the main experiment illustrates a specific weakness of the linear classifier for 'middle-ground' cases, not necessarily of the fingerprint itself, but this distinction relies on the authors' own follow-up pairwise experiment rather than independent verification.

datasets_used: German Wikipedia (via the HuggingFace wikimedia/wikipedia dataset, German portion) — source of 5-sentence excerpts for simplification generation and for the 10,000-excerpt rule-violation distribution analysis (Table 1)
826 unique source excerpts used to generate the 14,868-example simplification dataset across 18 configurations
A greedily-sampled, ~1,000-example evaluation subset constructed to guarantee coverage of rare comprehensibility-rule violations
quantitative_results: Pairwise model-size classification (Table 3): 1B vs 4B Acc 80.25/F1 79.77; 4B vs 12B Acc 68.96/F1 68.96; 1B vs 12B Acc 86.57/F1 86.04
Few-shot vs no-few-shot classification (Table 4): Rules Acc 63.22/F1 61.77; Content Acc 77.30/F1 77.30

Ablation removing Average Sentence Length (Table 5): Target F1 48.3→24.9; Rules F1 64.6→52.1; Plain F1 39.1→38.2; Content F1 67.1→67.4 (roughly unchanged); 1B F1 71.9→71.5; 4B F1 3.4→0.6; 12B F1 67.0→64.7 (note: source PDF Table 5's 12B accuracy cell appears to contain an OCR/typesetting artifact '(8.8 ± 0.8' rather than a clean number)

Comprehensibility rule-violation frequency across 10,000 sampled excerpts (Table 1): sentence length 84.71%, long words 72.99%, abstract words 70.68% (most frequent); anglicisms 12.57%, negation 10.16%, technical terms 3.37% (least frequent)
23 total Profiler metrics used as the LogisticRegression feature vector; Simple Baseline uses 6 length-related features
baselines_compared: Simple Baseline: LogisticRegression trained only on six basic length-related features

(Implicitly referenced, not re-implemented/benchmarked numerically in this paper) prior ATS evaluation approaches: BLEU, SARI, BERTScore, SAMSA, LENS, REFeREE, EASSE/EASSE-DE, LLM-as-a-Judge — discussed only in Related Work as motivation, not run as experimental baselines in this paper's own results
